system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 7: Chromatic Triads with Pedal and Alternating Bass

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 7 — Chromatic Triads with Pedal and Alternating Bass

_Pages 146-165._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Long reaches via relaxation

> The art of accomplishing long reaches is based on relaxed power control—not a large hand.

**Significance (p. 140).** States Van Eps's core thesis on extended fingerings: reach is a function of relaxed tendon control, not anatomy, which justifies prescribing wide-spread studies to all players.

## Develop reach gradually

> Reach must be developed gradually by patiently working with material that contains extended spreads. It must be practiced on the
> fingerboard under actual playing conditions—not forcing fingers apart with wedges etc.

**Significance (p. 140).** Rejects mechanical stretching aids in favor of in-context practice, defining the only sanctioned route to the extended spreads the method demands.

## Forcing shortens reach

> Forcing the fingers by tensing tightens the tendons and actually shortens the reach.

**Significance (p. 140).** Names the counter-productive failure mode (tensing) that students will instinctively use, framing relaxation as not just preferred but mechanically necessary.

## Chord symbol depends on use

> The G47°35 above is also an E diminished 9th-in other words the symbol for a chord depends on its
> use-its place in a progression etc.

**Significance (p. 141).** Establishes that Van Eps's chord labels are functional/contextual rather than absolute, which is essential for reading the substitution-heavy chromatic triad studies.

## Constructing all scales from chromatic exercises

> By using
> to up
>
> these chromatic exercises in all their various forms, positions, and combinations, all
> scales Maj., Min., Dim., Aug. may be constructed.

**Significance (p. 149).** Claims chromatic third-to-tenth exercises as a universal scale-construction engine, justifying the chapter's central drill as foundational rather than ornamental.

## Construct your own exercises

> Construct your own exercises by using portions of these forms, and all the other exercises in these
> books. use 1 note, 2 notes, 4 notes etc. Selected from the beginning, the middle or any place in the
> groups. Practise of this nature helps to increase reach and flexibility. If muscular discomfort is
> noticed, stop, rest the hand. Some discomfort is normal.

**Significance (p. 149).** Authorizes the student to fragment Van Eps's written exercises into custom drills, embedding generative practice as part of the method.

## Treat each measure separately

> Always Treat each measure as a sep@rate exercise-sustain the whole note for the full measure

**Significance (p. 152).** Defines the per-measure practice unit and the sustain-the-pedal rule that together give every chromatic triad station its lap-piano character.

## Sustained note describes the scale

> Notice that the sustained note is describing the C Major scale. All the

**Significance (p. 154).** Identifies the outer-voice anchor as a melodic scale carrier under the moving chromatic inner line—Van Eps's outer-voice-anchor principle in action.

## Same finger across half steps

> When working with long chromatic lines while sustaining a tone, the fingers have to sound two,
> sometimes three half steps in succession with the same finger, smoothly.

**Significance (p. 154).** Identifies the unusual single-finger chromatic walk required to keep the pedal sustained, a signature left-hand demand of Van Eps's chromatic work.

## Minor modes can't parallel major

> The Minor Modes Can't Parallel the Major because of the Minor third interval-it is shortened by one
>
> semitone

**Significance (p. 159).** Explains why minor-key chromatic-upper-voice studies require their own treatment rather than transposition of major-key forms, motivating the parallel minor exercises.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
